title: Listening - Part 3: The Battle for Your Brain
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a tech journalist, Sonia, and a digital psychologist, Thomas, about how apps are designed to keep us scrolling. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding 'infinite scroll,' dopamine loops, and the psychological cost of constant multi-tasking.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: If you aren't paying for the product, you *are* the product. This is the mantra of the attention economy. Sonia, how did 'attention' become the world’s most valuable currency?

Sonia: [15] It’s because the internet is no longer about information; it’s about 'engagement.' Advertisers don't just want you to see an ad; they want your time. [/15] The more minutes you spend on a platform, the more data they collect, and the more ads they can show you. We have moved from an economy of goods to an economy where your 'focus' is being harvested like oil.

Interviewer: Thomas, as a psychologist, you’ve looked at the design of these apps. What is 'infinite scroll'?

Thomas: [16] It’s a psychological trick based on 'variable rewards.' It’s the same mechanism used in slot machines. [/16] If you don't know what’s coming next, you keep pulling the lever—or in this case, scrolling down. Your brain is hunting for a 'dopamine hit' from a like, a comment, or a funny video. If the feed ended, you’d stop. By making it infinite, they bypass your brain’s 'stop' signal.

Interviewer: Sonia, is this why we find it so hard to read a book or watch a long movie now?

Sonia: [17] Precisely. We are training our brains to expect a reward every few seconds. Our 'deep attention'—the ability to focus on one thing for an hour—is being replaced by 'hyper-attention.' [/17] We are constantly scanning for the next stimulus. We feel 'bored' the moment there isn't a notification, but that 'boredom' is actually where creativity used to happen.

Interviewer: Thomas, what is the 'cost' of constant multi-tasking?

Thomas: [18] The truth is, humans can't actually multi-task. We 'switch-task.' Every time you look at a notification and then go back to work, there is a 'switching cost.' [/18] It takes your brain several minutes to get back to the same level of deep focus. If you do this all day, you end up feeling exhausted but like you’ve accomplished nothing.

Interviewer: Sonia, is 'digital detox' the answer?

Sonia: [19] It’s a temporary fix, like going on a juice fast after a month of eating junk food. The problem is systemic. [/19] We need 'humane technology'—apps that are designed to help us live our lives, not to steal our time. We need regulations that penalize companies for using 'dark patterns'—design choices that trick users into staying online longer than they intended.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Thomas? Can we win back our focus?

Thomas: [20] It starts with 'friction.' Turn off notifications, use a grayscale screen, or keep your phone in another room. You have to make it harder for the technology to win the battle for your brain. [/20]

Interviewer: Sonia, Thomas, thank you.

questions.question: Sonia says that in the 'attention economy', a user's focus is
options: A) something that companies try to protect at all costs.
B) a resource that is collected and sold like a raw material.
C) much less important than the quality of the information.
D) only valuable if the user is buying a specific product.
questions.explanation: She compares 'focus' to oil, being harvested and sold to advertisers.

questions.question: Thomas compares 'infinite scroll' to a slot machine because
options: A) it involves spending a lot of money very quickly.
B) it uses unpredictable rewards to keep people engaged.
C) it is only legal for people who are over eighteen.
D) it was originally designed to be used in casinos.
questions.explanation: He explains that 'variable rewards'—not knowing what comes next—keep people 'pulling the lever'.

questions.question: According to Sonia, 'hyper-attention' results in
options: A) a better ability to understand complex scientific books.
B) a constant search for new stimuli and a fear of boredom.
C) people becoming much more creative in their free time.
D) an increased memory for details in long movies.
questions.explanation: She says we are training our brains for quick rewards and scanning for the next stimulus.

questions.question: What does Thomas mean by 'switching cost'?
options: A) The price of buying a new smartphone every year.
B) The time it takes for the brain to refocus after a distraction.
C) The amount of data used when moving between different apps.
D) The energy used by the phone's battery during multi-tasking.
questions.explanation: He explains that every time you look at a notification, it takes minutes to return to 'deep focus'.

questions.question: Sonia's view on 'digital detoxes' is that they
options: A) are the only way to solve the problem of tech addiction.
B) should be forced on all employees by their companies.
C) only provide a short-term solution to a deeper problem.
D) are dangerous for people who work in the technology sector.
questions.explanation: She compares them to a 'juice fast' and says they are just a 'temporary fix'.

questions.question: Thomas suggests that people should use 'friction' to
options: A) improve the physical speed of their internet connection.
B) make it more difficult for them to access their phones.
C) encourage more people to buy the latest technology.
D) clean the screen of their devices more effectively.
questions.explanation: He suggests turning off notifications or keeping the phone in another room to make it harder to use.
